title: Chain-link Coptic stitch
subtitle: The Coptic variant that creates a long outer chain visible across the full spine
excerpt: The chain-link Coptic extends the outer loop of each stitch so it passes over two or three previous signatures before linking back. The result is a long horizontal chain pattern running across the full spine rather than a close herringbone. The book still opens completely flat, and the chain pattern is part of its decorative structure.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on Coptic and chain-link sewing structures. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bookbinding sewing technique where signatures are sewn together without adhesive. The thread links each signature to the previous one with a loop stitch, leaving the spine exposed and fully visible.
In Coptic-family bindings, a link stitch that spans two or more prior signature rows before looping back. The longer span creates a horizontal chain pattern visible from outside the spine.
A pierced hole through a signature through which the needle and thread pass during sewing.

content.text: Standard
Coptic stitch
links each signature to the previous one with a short outer loop. The
chain-link

variant extends that loop so that each pass on the outside of the spine crosses over two previous rows before looping back through the current signature. The external thread produces a visible horizontal chain along the full spine width. The sewing is otherwise identical to standard Coptic: the book opens completely flat, and no adhesive is needed.
What you need
Prepare eight to twelve signatures of equal size. Mark three
sewing stations

per signature: one at each kettle point 10 mm from head and tail, and one in the centre. Use the first signature as the template for all others, piercing through the stack with the awl in the piercing jig.
Sewing signatures 1 and 2
Thread the needle with waxed linen. Start inside signature 1 at the tail station. Pass out through the tail hole.
Enter the tail hole of signature 2 from outside, travel up the inside, and exit the head hole.
Pass under the external thread of signature 1 at the head station. Loop back through the head hole of signature 2 from outside inward.
Travel to the centre station inside signature 2 and exit.
The chain link, from signature 3 onward
The chain link differs from standard Coptic in that the outer exit loop passes over two previous signature threads, not just one, before looping back.
Enter signature 3 through the tail hole from outside. Travel to the head and exit.

On the outside: pass the needle under both the head-station thread of signature 2 and the head-station thread of signature 1 in a single loop. The needle tip passes below both threads, then up and back into the head hole of signature 3 from outside.
Travel to the centre and exit. Repeat the two-span loop at the centre station.
Travel to the tail and exit. Apply the two-span loop at the tail station before beginning the next signature.
Common mistakes

items.symptom: The chain pattern is irregular, some loops long, some short
items.cause: The loop-under is not consistent: it sometimes catches one previous row, sometimes two.
items.fix: Before pulling each stitch tight, confirm the needle tip has passed under exactly two previous thread runs. Count them visually before pulling.

items.symptom: Signatures gap open rather than lying flush
items.cause: Thread tension too loose, especially on the outer chain loops.
items.fix: After completing each station on the outside, hold the chain loop firmly with a fingertip and pull the working thread up against it before proceeding. The chain must be snug, not decoratively slack.

items.symptom: Thread tangles when reaching under two previous runs
items.cause: The needle is going under the threads in opposite directions on different signatures.
items.fix: Always pass the needle from the same direction on the outside, always from foredge toward the spine. Consistency of direction prevents cross-threading.
